Suits where the defendant(s) reside nearer to the county seat than to the city of the second class will be docketed and tried at the county seat, and process will indicate this.

classification.title: An Act for the Protection of Game Birds

Penalties are established for violations, and possession of protected birds is considered prima facie evidence of unlawful activity. The sale of protected birds during prohibited periods is also addressed.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 11 of the Acts of 1894, Relating to Graded Schools
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 11 of the Acts of 1894 concerning graded schools. It allows the board of trustees of graded schools to issue bonds to cover debts incurred for school buildings, grounds, furniture, and apparatus, and to impose an additional tax not exceeding twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars of property value to pay these bonds and interest. It also removes a restriction on issuing bonds for indebtedness contracted before the adoption of the current Constitution.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act providing for the creation and regulation of private corporations."
classification.summary: This act amends Section 203 of an existing act concerning private corporations. The amendment mandates that all companies keep their ticket offices open at least thirty minutes before the scheduled departure of passenger trains, and open waiting rooms at the same time. It also requires that delayed trains have the delay posted in the waiting room, and that station names be announced twice within each passenger car before arrival. Additionally, at junctions and crossings, the direction of travel for each car is to be announced.

It allows fiscal courts to subscribe to stock in incorporated turnpike road companies to aid in the purchase of existing roads. The act specifies limits on the amount of stock that can be subscribed per mile and outlines how the acquired interest in roads should be managed for the benefit of the traveling public. classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for widening public roads in Kentucky. It details how a county court can appoint surveyors to assess the necessity of widening a road, determine the required width, and estimate the cost and impact on adjacent landowners. It also specifies that portions of yards, lots, parks, or orchards can be taken for widening, but not graveyards without consent and proper reinterment provisions. Landowners whose land is taken are to be compensated.

It does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. It outlines the responsibilities of road supervisors regarding tools and implements, the process for approving expenditures, and the reporting requirements to the county court. It also details the collection and use of fines for road purposes and allows for the appointment of special commissioners for bridge construction or repair. It specifies that only paupers or those committed by court order after an acquittal of crime due to insanity are exempt from prepayment of six months' board and security. It also defines who qualifies as a pauper for the purposes of asylum admission and details how the asylum can recover costs from patients who acquire property.

The criteria for admission and payment are based on financial status (pauper status) and legal commitment, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Regulate the Sale of Spirituous, Vinous and Malt Liquors in Local Option Districts," Approved March 11, 1890
classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation regarding the sale of alcoholic beverages in local option districts. It clarifies exemptions for wholesale sales (not less than five gallons, not for consumption on premises) and for druggists. Druggists can sell for medical purposes only upon a physician's prescription, which must detail the date, quantity, and recipient. Physicians are prohibited from prescribing liquor unless absolutely required as medicine, with penalties for violations. Druggists must maintain a detailed register of all such sales, open for inspection by legal authorities. The act also exempts the procurement and use of liquors for sacramental purposes. Penalties are outlined for both physicians and druggists who fail to comply with these regulations.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of alcohol sales, specifically detailing exemptions for wholesale dealers, druggists selling for medical purposes via prescription, and for sacramental use. It outlines record-keeping requirements for druggists and penalties for violations by physicians and druggists. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or differentiate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb 1894.pdf' and the introductory text 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section Fifty-Nine and Sixty-Three of an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Organization and Government of Common Schools," Approved May 23, 1890
classification.summary: This act amends sections 59 and 63 of an existing education law. Section 59 is amended to clarify the process for appointing a successor to a county superintendent in case of vacancy, including provisions for the duration of the appointed term and the notification of the Superintendent of Public Instruction for examinations. It also outlines the grounds and appeal process for removing a county superintendent. Section 63 is amended to require county superintendents to immediately deliver any money, property, books, effects, or papers to their successor or the county court clerk upon leaving office, and to settle with the county court within ten days, with a penalty for failure. The amendment also states that county certificates shall be granted by the county.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section Eighty-Nine of the Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This act amends section eighty-nine of the Kentucky Statutes, detailing the duties of school trustees. It outlines their responsibilities in examining teacher registers, assessing school conditions, managing attendance, addressing school needs, and enforcing regulations. The act also grants trustees the power to suspend or expel pupils upon a teacher's complaint and allows for the dismissal of a teacher if average daily attendance falls below 25% of the district's pupil children for twenty consecutive days, unless the decrease is due to natural causes. Furthermore, it specifies that trustees can be removed from office by the county superintendent for neglect of duty, misfeasance, or malfeasance, granting the superintendent subpoena and attachment powers for investigations.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administrative and disciplinary aspects of public education, specifically the roles and powers of school trustees and county superintendents. It details procedures for managing school resources, student attendance, and teacher performance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language used is general and applies to all students and teachers within the school district. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend article two of chapter sixty-five of Session Acts one thousand eight hundred and ninety-one-ninety-two-ninety-three, entitled "An act to regulate elections in this Commonwealth," and to extend said articles.
classification.summary: This act amends existing election regulations by adding a new section to Article Two of Chapter Sixty-Five of the 1891-1892-1893 Session Acts. It mandates that in towns or cities of the fifth or sixth class that span across two or more counties, the county court clerk of each respective county must prepare a separate ballot-book for municipal elections. These ballots will only contain candidates for municipal offices or questions submitted to the voters of that specific town or city, and will be prepared in accordance with existing election laws.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative process of preparing ballots for municipal elections in towns or cities that are located in multiple counties. It outlines the responsibilities of county clerks in preparing separate ballot books for voters within their respective county portions of such municipalities. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act relating to revenue and taxation,"
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning revenue and taxation. It modifies sections related to the lien on property for taxes due, stating that such liens cannot be defeated by gift, devise, sale, or alienation unless made more than five years prior to enforcement proceedings. It also clarifies that nothing is exempt from levy and sale for taxes and associated costs. Additionally, it allows for retrospective assessment of property not assessed in a given year, up to five years later, without prejudicing the rights of purchasers acquired in the meantime. It also mandates the sheriff or Auditor's agent to list omitted property for taxation.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 52 of the General Statutes, Relating to Husband and Wife
classification.summary: This act details the property rights and contractual abilities of married women in Kentucky. It clarifies that a married woman's separate estate is not liable for her husband's debts, but is liable for her own pre-marital debts and those incurred after marriage, with exceptions. It grants married women the right to acquire, hold, sell, and dispose of personal property independently, and to make contracts and sue or be sued as if unmarried. However, it restricts her ability to executory contract to sell, convey, or mortgage real estate unless her husband joins. She retains the right to rent out her real estate and collect rents. Gifts, transfers, or assignments of personal property between spouses are invalid against third parties unless in writing, acknowledged, and recorded. The husband is generally not liable for his wife's debts, except to the value of property received from her, but is liable for necessaries. The act also addresses situations where the husband abandons the wife.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided discusses the legal rights and responsibilities of married women concerning property and contracts. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.summary: This statute outlines provisions for married women to sell their real estate under specific circumstances, such as abandonment or imprisonment of the husband. It also details the survivor's life estate in one-third of the deceased spouse's real estate and an absolute estate in one-half of the personalty. Additionally, it addresses forfeiture of rights due to adultery and conditions under which a wife may have dower in land her husband was seized of in law. It also specifies lands from which a wife shall not be endowed.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with property rights of married women and inheritance, which are civil matters. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb 1894.pdf' and the page numbering '178 LAWS OF KENTUCKY'. The category is 'marriage' as the statute primarily concerns the legal rights and property of spouses within a marriage.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1894 details provisions related to a wife's dower rights and jointure. It outlines how a wife's interest in her husband's property is affected by conveyances, devises, or jointures, and specifies procedures for waiving jointures and claiming dower. It also addresses indemnity for a wife deprived of her jointure and her entitlement to rents and profits from her husband's dowable real estate until dower is assigned, including the right to occupy the mansion-house and its appurtenances.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The language used is general and applies to all individuals within the legal framework of the time regarding marital property. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because the text is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and includes section numbers typical of legislative acts passed during a session.

classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1894 details various aspects of marital property rights, dower rights, and divorce. It covers situations such as recovery of rent after death, barring dower by default or collusion, equitable allotment of dower, dower in executory contracts, survivorship rights in property conveyed to husband and wife, and the effect of divorce on property claims. It also addresses the sale of a wife's inchoate right of dower when she is a confirmed lunatic.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 51 of Chapter 47 of the General Statutes, relating to the conveyance of real estate by married women.
classification.summary: This act amends existing law regarding the conveyance of real estate by married women, particularly when the husband is an infant, idiot, lunatic, or imbecile. It outlines procedures for the wife to join in the conveyance to release her dower rights, either with a guardian/committee or a court-appointed commissioner. It also addresses situations where a wife not of full age may convey with her adult husband.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided discusses legal procedures for married women to convey their husband's real estate, specifically in cases where the husband is incapacitated (infant, idiot, lunatic, imbecile). It details the roles of guardians, committees, and court commissioners in such transactions and the wife's dower rights. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language and subject matter are purely related to property law and marital rights. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the appointment of an Inspector of Mines, and to define his duties, and to prescribe his compensation."

It outlines the Curator's duties, including managing records, responding to inquiries about mineral resources, distributing maps and reports, and potentially overseeing the printing of unpublished reports. The Inspector/Curator will receive fifty dollars per month for these extra duties and must provide a bond. The compensation begins upon the act's approval, and an emergency is declared for immediate effect.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the provisions of the Civil Code of Practice concerning injunctions.
classification.summary: This act amends sections 271 and 273 of the Civil Code of Practice concerning injunctions. It repeals section 271 and enacts a new section that clarifies when an injunction can be a final judgment or a provisional remedy, and how mandatory injunctions should be directed. It also abolishes the writ of injunction. Section 273 is amended to detail who can grant an injunction (court, circuit judge, clerk, county judge, or justices of the peace under specific absence conditions) and the requirements for granting such injunctions, including the necessity of filing a petition and an affidavit stating the injunction has not been refused by a higher authority.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to civil procedure regarding injunctions. It details the process and conditions under which injunctions can be granted or modified within the legal system. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the 'General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky' in 1894.

classification.title: Amendments to the Civil Code of Practice regarding injunctions
classification.summary: This statute amends sections 286 and 747 of the Civil Code of Practice. Section 286 is amended to clarify the punishment for disobedience of an injunction, allowing for contempt charges and potential jail time or bond. Section 747 is amended to state that an appeal does not stay proceedings on a judgment unless a supersedeas is issued, and that these provisions do not apply to judgments granting, modifying, perpetuating, or dissolving injunctions. The court may, at its discretion, suspend proceedings on an injunction judgment during an appeal if the ends of justice require it.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details amendments to the Civil Code of Practice concerning the enforcement and appeal of injunctions. It outlines procedures for handling disobedience of injunctions and the effect of appeals on injunction judgments. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to be procedural and applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate and insure the assessment of property for taxation, and the payment of taxes thereon, belonging to non-residents of the counties in which the same is situated.
classification.summary: This act requires individuals owning property (lands, mineral rights, standing trees, coal, oil, or gas privileges) in a county other than their residence, or if they reside out of state, to file a descriptive list of this property with the county clerk by August 15, 1894. This list must include details like the number and kind of trees, the location of lands and mineral rights, and the nearest resident to each tract. The assessor will then use this list to assess the property for taxes at its fair cash value. Failure to comply will result in a fine of one hundred dollars and costs for each offense.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the taxation of property owned by non-residents within Kentucky. It outlines procedures for reporting and assessing such property to ensure tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is solely on residency and property ownership for tax purposes. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section Thirty-one of Chapter Thirty-two of the General Statutes, and Section Twelve of Article Five of an Act Entitled "An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter One Hundred and Twenty-nine of the Acts of the General Assembly of Kentucky, Approved March 17, 1870, and All Acts Amendatory Thereto," Approved March 21, 1890.
classification.summary: This act amends two sections of Kentucky law. The first amendment (to Section 31) clarifies the powers of a town's board of trustees regarding public works, including the financing of street and sewer construction/repair, and sidewalk/curbing expenses. The second amendment (to Section 12 of Article 5) modifies eligibility for town offices, stating that no person can hold an office except that of marshal unless they are a resident and elector of the town and have resided there for one year preceding election or appointment. The marshal must also be a resident when appointed or elected.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details amendments to existing Kentucky laws concerning municipal governance and public works. The first amendment clarifies the financial responsibilities for town improvements, such as streets, sewers, sidewalks, and curbing, and how these costs are to be borne by the town or property owners. The second amendment addresses eligibility requirements for holding town offices, specifically requiring residency and elector status for most positions, with a similar requirement for the town marshal. Neither amendment explicitly mentions race, nor do they contain provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language used is procedural and administrative, relating to the functioning of local government and public infrastructure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act for the Government of Towns of the Sixth Class
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the government of towns of the sixth class. It modifies provisions related to filling vacancies in the board of trustees and the frequency of police court sessions for civil cases. Specifically, it allows the county judge to fill vacancies on the board of trustees by appointment until the next regular election, and it increases the number of civil court sessions from four times a year at three-month intervals to twelve times a year at one-month intervals.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and judicial procedures for towns of the sixth class in Kentucky. It addresses the process of filling vacancies in the town's board of trustees and the scheduling of civil court sessions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The changes are procedural and administrative, affecting all residents of these towns equally. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes counties in Kentucky to remove stagnant water and reclaim swamp lands to improve public health. Counties can undertake these projects by ditching, leveeing, or cleaning out debris that causes water to pool. The costs are to be paid from the county levy or by taxation. If the cost exceeds a county's annual income, an election can be held to ascertain the will of the voters regarding the improvement.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 18 and Section 27 of an Act Entitled "An Act to Regulate the Summoning of Jurors and the Drawing of Grand and Petit Juries in the County Courts of this Commonwealth," Approved May 10, 1893.
classification.summary: This act amends sections 18 and 27 of a previous act concerning jury selection. It reduces the number of peremptory challenges in civil actions from one-third of the jury summoned to three jurors. It also changes the number of names drawn from the box for jury selection in civil cases from twenty to eighteen, and reduces the number of names each party can strike from four to three.

classification.title: An act concerning juries
classification.summary: This act amends an existing law concerning juries by adding exemptions for notaries public and trustees of schools, and clarifying who is not compelled to serve on a petit jury. It also states that the fact of an incompetent person serving on a jury shall not be cause for setting aside a verdict or taking exceptions after the jury has been sworn. The act declares an emergency and goes into effect upon passage.

classification.title: Extension of limits

The statute also details procedures for filling vacancies in city offices.

classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for extending the corporate limits of cities of the second class in Kentucky. The focus is on annexing densely populated, built-up areas and excluding agricultural lands.

It outlines procedural requirements for annexation, including voter approval for unincorporated territories and a preference for annexing densely populated areas over agricultural lands. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to be purely administrative and territorial in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The tax is a lien on the property, but the amount assessed against any lot or land cannot exceed one dollar per front foot. The statute also begins to outline the executive department, specifically the mayor's role, election, term, and qualifications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 33 of the General Statutes, Relating to Police Courts in Cities of the First, Second and Third Class
classification.summary: This statute outlines the jurisdiction and powers of police courts in cities of the first, second, and third class in Kentucky. It grants these courts jurisdiction over cases of petit larceny and vagrancy, requiring justices of the peace to direct recognizances for such offenses to the police court. Convicted individuals are to be sent to labor in the city prison or workhouse for a period of three to twelve months. The statute also details the police judge's jurisdiction over riots, routs, and unlawful assemblies, empowering them to issue warrants for arrest. Importantly, it states that presentment or indictment by a grand jury is not necessary for vagrancy or petit larceny, allowing for arrest on warrant and trial by the police judge. Women can be tried and convicted of vagrancy under this statute.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the jurisdiction and procedures of police courts concerning minor offenses like petit larceny and vagrancy, and public order offenses like riots and unlawful assemblies. While vagrancy laws were often used to control Black populations during the Jim Crow era, this specific text does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. The penalties and procedures described apply generally to all individuals within the specified cities, regardless of race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an 'Act' from the 'Laws of Kentucky' in 1894.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 283
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1894 details procedural aspects of a city court. It outlines jury selection (six jurors with circuit court qualifications), the role of police officers as court officers, the court's ability to assess costs up to three dollars for non-payment of fines, the collection and remittance of fines and costs, and the judge's duty to keep a register of replevin bonds and enforce their payment. It also defines the territorial jurisdiction of the court as coextensive with the city limits and extending to the low water mark on the Ohio River for cities bordering it.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment of property in cities of the second, third and fourth classes."
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for a board of equalization in cities of the second, third, and fourth classes. It outlines their powers to inspect assessment books, interrogate city officials, administer oaths, compel witness attendance, and hear complaints regarding property assessments. The board can increase or decrease assessments to ensure uniformity and true valuation, with specific notice requirements for individual increases and a general notice by publication for uniform percentage changes. The board's session is limited to four weeks, with three members constituting a quorum.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the functions and powers of a board of equalization responsible for property tax assessments in certain classes of Kentucky cities. It details procedures for reviewing assessments, hearing complaints, and making adjustments to ensure uniformity and accurate valuation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to revise and consolidate the charter of the city of Louisville," approved March 25, 1890, and to amend the charter of said city, and to provide for the government of said city.
classification.summary: This section of the statute outlines the powers of the city council regarding tax collection. It allows the city solicitor to act as a delinquent tax collector, with the power to distrain and sell goods and chattels to recover unpaid taxes. It also details procedures for reporting collections and making settlements with the auditor. Section 16 grants the general council the authority to modify or change the manner of property assessment, levy, and collection of taxes, provided a two-thirds majority vote in each board. Article IX establishes the mayor and presidents of the boards of aldermen and councilmen as commissioners of the sinking fund.

classification.title: Laws of Kentucky, 1894, Chapter 113, Article X
classification.summary: This section of the 1894 Laws of Kentucky, specifically Article X, outlines general provisions for ordinances and legal rights. It states that existing ordinances not inconsistent with the act will remain in force, and that the repeal of any law will not affect acquired rights or liabilities. It also clarifies that rights of action, fines, and forfeitures accrued before the act takes effect remain unaffected. Finally, it repeals ordinances for street or alley openings where proceedings haven't commenced, while continuing others in force.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the 1894 Laws of Kentucky, specifically Article X, deals with general provisions regarding the continuation of existing ordinances, the effect of repeals on acquired rights and liabilities, and the status of accrued rights of action, fines, and forfeitures. It also addresses the repeal and continuation of ordinances related to street and alley openings. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 113 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Public Education in Cities of the Second Class
classification.summary: This act outlines the election and qualifications of members for the board of education in cities of the second class. It specifies that members are elected from each ward, with terms of two years. It also details the election of a president from the board, voting procedures (secret ballot), and eligibility requirements for both board members and voters. Notably, it states that women possessing the same qualifications as males are eligible for board membership and voting.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the election and qualifications for members of a city's board of education. It mentions provisions for women's eligibility to vote and serve on the board, which was a progressive aspect for the time. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act from the General Assembly of 1894. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on electoral and eligibility rules without any racial component.

classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful for any person to knowingly or willfully keep plum, cherry, or other trees infected with the contagious disease known as "black knot." classification.summary: This act authorizes the judge of the criminal division of circuit courts in counties with a population of seventy-five thousand or more to appoint an official interpreter. The interpreter must be fluent in English and German, present at all sessions, and interpret statements when directed by the judge. They will take an oath of office and receive an annual salary not exceeding six hundred dollars, paid from the county treasury.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment of official interpreters in circuit courts in populous counties. It specifies language requirements (English and German) and duties related to interpreting in criminal cases. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Article 3, Chapter 103, Session Acts 1891-1892-1893, relating to reports of turnpike road companies.
classification.summary: This act requires all turnpike road companies in Kentucky to submit detailed financial reports annually to county clerks. These reports, to be filled out on blanks provided by the Auditor, will include the company's financial condition, total road length, and length within each county. The county board of supervisors will use these reports to assess the value of the turnpike franchise for taxation purposes, adding it to the tangible property assessment. Failure to comply or knowingly submitting false information constitutes a misdemeanor, punishable by a fine.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the reporting and taxation of turnpike road companies. It outlines administrative procedures for financial reporting and franchise valuation. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the organization and government of cities of the first class," approved March 17, 1894.
classification.summary: This act details the powers and procedures of the board of public safety in cities of the first class, including the process for removing members of the police force, the issuance of subpoenas, and the enforcement of city ordinances. It specifies that police officers cannot be removed without written charges, examination, and a hearing, unless they become insane or unfit for duty. classification.summary: This statute outlines the structure and appointment process for the police force in first-class cities in Kentucky. It details the ranks of officers, including majors, captains, lieutenants, sergeants, detectives, and patrolmen, and specifies the maximum number of individuals allowed in each rank. The Board of Public Safety is given the authority to appoint all members of the police force and to increase the number of patrolmen if appropriations are made. It also describes the establishment and function of a detective force, with the ability to reassign detectives to patrol duties.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1894 details the organizational structure and disciplinary powers of a police force. It outlines the promotion hierarchy within the police department (patrolman to major) and grants the board of public safety broad authority to punish members for various offenses, including neglect of duty, violation of rules, absence without leave, immoral conduct, and other breaches of discipline. The board can impose punishments such as reprimand, forfeiture of pay, suspension, or dismissal. It also allows for deductions in pay for absences, sick time, or other disabilities. Furthermore, the statute prohibits legal action by police members against the city or its officials to recover forfeited or deducted salary.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 109 of the General Statutes, Relating to Cities of the First, Second, Third and Fourth Classes
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal process for annexing or detaching territory from cities of the first, second, third, and fourth classes. It details the requirements for filing a defense against such changes, the court's role in hearing the case without a jury, and the conditions under which the proposed annexation or detachment will be approved or denied. The statute specifies that the court's decision is based on whether a majority of resident voters have remonstrated and whether the change would cause material injury to property owners. It also sets a two-year waiting period before a denied proposal can be resubmitted and states that no appeal can be made from the circuit court's judgment.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes a legal procedure for city boundary changes. It focuses on the rights of resident voters and property owners to object to these changes and the court's role in adjudicating such disputes. There is no mention of race, and the criteria for objection (majority of voters, material injury to property owners) are not inherently tied to racial discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 84 of an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Improvement of Public Ways in Cities of the First Class," Approved March 18, 1890
classification.summary: This act amends section 84 of a previous act concerning street improvements in first-class cities. It establishes a lien for the cost of original improvements to public ways, market spaces, public squares, grounds, wharves, levees, and sidewalks. The lien takes effect from the date of the ordinance ordering the improvement, accrues six percent annual interest, and is superior to all other liens. Payment can be enforced by suit in equity, and errors in council proceedings will not exempt property owners from payment or defeat the lien after the work is done. The act also details how assessments can be paid off in full or apportioned when property is divided.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the process and legal framework for funding and enforcing street improvements in first-class cities. It details the establishment of liens, payment schedules, and legal recourse for collecting assessments. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends provisions related to municipal finance and taxation for cities of the fourth class in Kentucky. Section 8 limits the amount a city can charge property owners for street improvements to one-half the value of the ground after improvement, excluding buildings. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment of property for taxation, and for the collection of taxes thereon," approved March 15, 1878.

It specifies that the county clerk shall furnish the assessor with a list of all persons and property within the county, and that the assessor shall assess all property at its fair cash value. It also details the process for appealing assessments and the penalties for failing to list property. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory provisions.
